refsect1.title: Description
refsect1.para: git-crypt enables transparent encryption and decryption of files in a git repository. Files which you choose to protect are encrypted when committed, and decrypted when checked out. git-crypt lets you freely share a repository containing a mix of public and private content. git-crypt gracefully degrades, so developers without the secret key can still clone and commit to a repository with encrypted files. This lets you store your secret material (such as keys or passwords) in the same repository as your code, without requiring you to lock down your entire repository.

refsect1.title: Commands
refsect1.para: git-crypt is logically divided into several sub-commands which perform distinct tasks. Each sub-command, and its arguments, are documented below. Note that arguments and options to sub-commands must be specified on the command line after the name of the sub-command.

listitem.para: Generate a key and prepare the current Git repository to use git-crypt.
The following options are understood:
Initialize the given key instead of the default key. git-crypt supports multiple keys per repository, allowing you to share different files with different sets of collaborators.
Force re-initialization even if the repository is already configured for git-crypt.
Display a list of files in the repository, with their status (encrypted or unencrypted).
The following options are understood:
Show only encrypted files.
Show only unencrypted files.

Encrypt files that should be encrypted but were committed to the repository or added to the index without encryption. (This can happen if a file is added before git-crypt is initialized or before the file is added to the gitattributes file.)
Output in machine-parseable TSV format (path, encrypted, filter_installed, key_name) suitable for scripting.

Add the users with the given GPG user IDs as collaborators. Specifically, git-crypt uses gpg1 to encrypt the shared symmetric key to the public keys of each GPG user ID, and stores the GPG-encrypted keys in the .git-crypt directory at the root of the repository.
GPG_USER_ID can be a key ID, a full fingerprint, an email address, or anything else that uniquely identifies a public key to GPG (see "HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID" in the gpg1 man page).
The following options are understood:
Grant access to the given key, rather than the default key.
Don't automatically commit the changes to the .git-crypt directory.
Assume that the GPG keys specified on the command line are trusted; i.e. they actually belong to the users that they claim to belong to.
Without this option, git-crypt uses the same trust model as GPG, which is based on the Web of Trust by default. Under this model, git-crypt will reject GPG keys that do not have trusted signatures.

If you don't want to use the Web of Trust, you can either change GPG's trust model by setting the trust-model option in ~/.gnupg/gpg.conf (see gpg1), or use the --trusted option to add-gpg-user on a case-by-case basis.

Decrypt the repository. If one or more key files are specified on the command line, git-crypt attempts to decrypt using those shared symmetric keys. If no key files are specified, git-crypt attempts to decrypt using a GPG-encrypted key stored in the repository's .git-crypt directory.
This command takes no options.
Export the repository's shared symmetric key to the given file.
The following options are understood:
Export the given key, rather than the default key.

Revoke access for the GPG user with the given user ID. Removes the GPG-encrypted key file from the .git-crypt directory. After removing a user, you should run rotate-key to generate a new key version that the removed user cannot decrypt.
Revoke access to the given key, rather than the default key.
List all GPG user fingerprints that have access to encrypted files, organized by key name.
List users for the given key only, rather than all keys.
Add an age recipient as a collaborator. The symmetric key is encrypted (wrapped) to the recipient's age public key and stored in the .git-crypt directory. Requires the age binary to be installed.
Grant access to the given key, rather than the default key.
Add an SSH public key as a recipient. The SSH key file (e.g. ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub) is converted to an age-compatible recipient. Supports ssh-ed25519 and ssh-rsa key types.
Don't automatically commit the changes.
Revoke access for the given age recipient. After removing a recipient, run rotate-key to generate a new key version.
Revoke access to the given key, rather than the default key.

Generate a new key version and re-encrypt all files. All existing GPG users and age recipients are automatically re-wrapped with the new key version. Use this after removing a collaborator to ensure they cannot decrypt future content.
Rotate the given key, rather than the default key.
Split the symmetric key into M-of-N Shamir shares. Any M shares can reconstruct the key, but fewer than M shares reveal no information about the key (information-theoretic security).
Total number of shares to generate.
Minimum number of shares required to reconstruct the key.
Split the given key, rather than the default key.
Force re-checkout of all encrypted files. Useful after key changes, filter updates, or when encrypted files appear corrupted in the working copy.
Install a pre-commit hook that checks for accidental plaintext commits of files that should be encrypted. The hook verifies that the git-crypt filter is properly applied before allowing a commit.
Verify that all commits which modified encrypted files are GPG or SSH signed. Useful for auditing repository integrity and detecting unauthorized modifications.
Generate a .sops.yaml configuration file for use with Mozilla SOPS. This bridges git-crypt with SOPS for structured file encryption (YAML, JSON, ENV files).
Create a .credentials/ directory with encryption pre-configured via .gitattributes. Provides a standardized location for storing encrypted credentials.

Display the cryptographic audit trail. Each entry in the log is hash-chained (SHA-256) to the previous entry, providing tamper detection. The log records encryption and decryption events with timestamps and user identifiers.
Verify the integrity of the audit log hash chain. Checks that each entry's hash correctly references the previous entry, detecting any tampering or corruption.

Publish the current audit log hash to a blockchain for immutable timestamping. This creates an on-chain record that can be used to prove the audit log existed in its current state at a specific point in time.

Add an Ethereum wallet address as a collaborator. The wallet's signature is used to derive an age-compatible X25519 key, which is then used to wrap the symmetric key. This enables decentralized identity-based access control without traditional key management.
Display help for the given COMMAND, or an overview of all commands if no command is specified.
Print the currently-installed version of git-crypt. The format of the output is always "git-crypt", followed by a space, followed by the dotted version number.

refsect1.title: Using git-crypt
refsect1.para: First, you prepare a repository to use git-crypt by running git-crypt init.
Then, you specify the files to encrypt by creating a gitattributes5 file. Each file which you want to encrypt should be assigned the "filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt" attributes. For example:

Like a .gitignore file, .gitattributes files can match wildcards and should be checked into the repository. Make sure you don't accidentally encrypt the .gitattributes file itself (or other git files like .gitignore or .gitmodules). Make sure your .gitattributes rules are in place before you add sensitive files, or those files won't be encrypted!
To share the repository with others (or with yourself) using GPG, run:

GPG_USER_ID can be a key ID, a full fingerprint, an email address, or anything else that uniquely identifies a public key to GPG. Note: git-crypt add-gpg-user will add and commit a GPG-encrypted key file in the .git-crypt directory of the root of your repository.
Alternatively, you can share access using age (a modern, simpler alternative to GPG):
Or using an existing SSH public key:
You can also export a symmetric secret key, which you must securely convey to collaborators (GPG/age is not required, and no files are added to your repository):
After cloning a repository with encrypted files, unlock with GPG:
Or with age:
Or with a symmetric key:
That's all you need to do - after git-crypt is set up (either with git-crypt init or git-crypt unlock), you can use git normally - encryption and decryption happen transparently.

refsect1.title: The .gitattributes file
refsect1.para: The .gitattributes file is documented in gitattributes5. The file pattern format is the same as the one used by .gitignore, as documented in gitignore5, with the exception that specifying merely a directory (e.g. "/dir/") is not sufficient to encrypt all files beneath it.
Also note that the pattern "dir/*" does not match files under sub-directories of dir/. To encrypt an entire sub-tree dir/, place the following in dir/.gitattributes:
The second pattern is essential for ensuring that .gitattributes itself is not encrypted.

refsect1.title: Multiple Key Support
refsect1.para: In addition to the implicit default key, git-crypt supports alternative keys which can be used to encrypt specific files and can be shared with specific GPG users. This is useful if you want to grant different collaborators access to different sets of files.
To generate an alternative key named KEYNAME, pass the -k KEYNAME option to git-crypt init as follows:
To encrypt a file with an alternative key, use the git-crypt-KEYNAME filter in .gitattributes as follows:
To export an alternative key or share it with a GPG user, pass the -k KEYNAME option to git-crypt export-key or git-crypt add-gpg-user as follows:
refsect1.screen: git-crypt export-key -k KEYNAME /path/to/keyfile git-crypt add-gpg-user -k KEYNAME GPG_USER_ID
refsect1.para: To unlock a repository with an alternative key, use git-crypt unlock normally. git-crypt will automatically determine which key is being used.

refsect1.title: Examples

para.emphasis: Initialize a repository and encrypt files:

refsect1.screen: $ cd my-repo $ git-crypt init Generating key... $ echo 'secret.conf filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt' >> .gitattributes $ echo '*.key filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt' >> .gitattributes $ git add .gitattributes $ git commit -m "Configure git-crypt encryption" $ echo "API_KEY=s3cret" > secret.conf $ git add secret.conf $ git commit -m "Add encrypted configuration"
$ git-crypt add-gpg-user alice@example.com $ git-crypt add-gpg-user --trusted bob@example.com $ git-crypt add-gpg-user -k staging deploy@example.com

# Lock the repo (encrypt all files in the working copy) $ git-crypt lock
# Unlock with GPG (requires your private key) $ git-crypt unlock
# Unlock with a symmetric key file $ git-crypt unlock /path/to/keyfile

# Show all files and their encryption status $ git-crypt status
# Show only encrypted files $ git-crypt status -e
# Fix files that should be encrypted but aren't $ git-crypt status -f

para.emphasis: Export and share a symmetric key:

refsect1.screen:
# Export the default key $ git-crypt export-key /path/to/shared-key
# Export a named key $ git-crypt export-key -k staging /path/to/staging-key
# Export a specific key version $ git-crypt export-key --version 2 /path/to/key-v2
# A collaborator can then unlock with the exported key $ git-crypt unlock /path/to/shared-key

# Add an age recipient $ git-crypt add-age-recipient age1ql3z7hjy54pw3hyww5ayyfg7zqgvc7w3j2elw8zmrj2kg5sfn9aqmcac8p
# Add a collaborator by their SSH public key $ git-crypt add-age-recipient --ssh ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
# Unlock with age identity $ git-crypt unlock --age
# Unlock with a specific age identity file $ git-crypt unlock --age --identity /path/to/age-key.txt
# Remove an age recipient $ git-crypt rm-age-recipient age1ql3z7hjy54pw3hyww5ayyfg7zqgvc7w3j2elw8zmrj2kg5sfn9aqmcac8p

# List all GPG users with access $ git-crypt ls-gpg-users
# Remove a GPG user's access $ git-crypt rm-gpg-user alice@example.com
# Rotate the key after removing a user $ git-crypt rotate-key

para.emphasis: Split a key using Shamir's Secret Sharing:

refsect1.screen:
# Split into 5 shares, requiring 3 to reconstruct $ git-crypt split-key -n 5 -m 3
# Unlock using 3 of the 5 shares $ git-crypt unlock --shares share1.key share2.key share3.key

# Install pre-commit hook to prevent plaintext leaks $ git-crypt install-hooks
# Verify all commits touching encrypted files are signed $ git-crypt verify-commits
# View the cryptographic audit trail $ git-crypt audit-log
# Verify audit log integrity $ git-crypt verify-audit
# Anchor audit hash to blockchain $ git-crypt anchor-audit

# Generate .sops.yaml for structured file encryption $ git-crypt sops-config
# Set up a .credentials/ directory with encryption $ git-crypt credentials-init

refsect1.title: See Also
refsect1.para: git1, gitattributes5, gpg1, age encryption, Mozilla SOPS, git-crypt-revived GitHub repository
